heroTagline: Men aren't good at asking for help. Most were never taught how — only that they shouldn't need it.

sections.heading: The island looks like safety
sections.body:
Somewhere along the way, most men learn to handle pain the same way: alone. Swallow it, outwork it, joke around it, pour something over it. Isolation feels protective — nobody sees you struggle, nobody gets to judge, nothing gets vulnerable.

But the island that looks like safety works like a trap. The things you never say don't dissolve. They compound — quietly, with interest.

sections.heading: What the silence costs
sections.body:
Left alone, the same patterns keep cycling: the anger that flares over small things, the numbness you call being tired, the habits that promise relief and deliver shame. The cost lands on the people closest to you — a wife who has stopped asking what's wrong, kids who study your moods like a weather report.

And there's a quieter cost underneath it: distance from God. Not a dramatic loss of faith — just a slow drift from the source of the strength you keep trying to generate on your own. Unaddressed, these patterns don't stay contained to one man. They get handed down.

sections.heading: You were built for connection
sections.body:
Scripture is blunt about this: it is not good for man to be alone. That was never just about marriage — it's about how men are made. Iron sharpens iron. Burdens are meant to be carried by more than one set of shoulders.

Counseling isn't a substitute for that kind of life. It's how a lot of men find their way back to it.

sections.heading: More than putting out fires
sections.body:
Some men come in because something is on fire — a marriage, a job, a habit that finally cost too much. That's a good reason to come. But this work isn't only for crisis.

Plenty of men come to grow: to lead their families with steadiness instead of reactivity, to repair what their temper or absence damaged, to figure out who they are now that a season has ended, or to finally deal with something they've dragged behind them for twenty years. Whatever brought you here, the work is the same — honest, purposeful, and aimed at lasting change.

sections.heading: What I'm here for
sections.body:
I'm not here to lecture you, fix you, or hand you a worksheet. I'm a licensed counselor who works exclusively with men, and my job is to walk alongside you — asking the questions no one else asks, telling you the truth when it would be easier not to, and helping you see your story in light of God's redemptive one.

You've carried it this far. You don't have to carry it further alone.

faqs.question: I've never talked to a counselor. What if it's not for me?
faqs.answer: Fair question — plenty of men who sit in my office said the same thing. There's no performance required. We start with what's actually going on, at your pace. Most men find that talking to a counselor is less like being analyzed and more like finally thinking out loud with someone who knows how to help.

faqs.question: Do I have to be a Christian to come?
faqs.answer: No. My counseling is grounded in a Biblical framework, and faith is woven into my work — but you set the depth. Men at every point of belief and doubt are welcome here.

faqs.question: Do you only work with men?
faqs.answer: Yes — men and boys ages 12 and up. Focusing the practice this way lets me do this one thing well. If you're looking for counseling for a woman in your life, I'm glad to point you to trusted counselors in the area.
